content.text: This page consolidates the requirements and recommended operating model for
The goal is to provide a well-structured foundation for maintaining templates as governed, continuously improving intelligence assets rather than static files.
AI-driven review, optimization, and improvement loops
The recommendations below are grounded in the current ADPA backend and service architecture, including:
supporting template and database search paths
ADPA already treats templates as dynamic system assets that evolve based on generated output quality, audit findings, and optimization opportunities.
2. Versioning is split across templates and documents
is aware of template version changes and can force a larger document version bump when a material template change occurs
3. AI feedback loops are already present
ADPA contains a structured loop across multiple services:
performs multi-dimensional audits of generated outputs
identifies regressions and proposes optimization actions
identifies recurring patterns and proposes broader improvements
manages regeneration behavior in response to template evolution
4. Human review is currently a core safety control
Optimization suggestions are not automatically pushed into production. The current architecture uses a
where suggestions remain pending until explicitly approved.
Templates should move through explicit lifecycle states.
tracked by quality and performance metrics
ready to apply to production template version
no longer preferred for new generation
each template must have a clear ownership model
each lifecycle transition must be auditable
active templates must always have a recoverable previous version
deprecated and archived templates must remain traceable to generated documents
lifecycle changes must preserve compatibility history
increment template version on every applied optimization or approved structural change
store change rationale, source of recommendation, approver, and timestamp per version
continue using semantic versioning for generated documents
define version bump policy based on template impact:
: structural or semantic intent change caused by template update
: meaningful content improvement without major structural change
: typo, formatting, or low-risk corrective update
every generated document must retain a reference to:
if a template update materially changes document structure or required interpretation, regeneration should force a
regenerated documents should preserve lineage to prior document versions
The audit layer should remain the canonical evaluator of generation quality.
Based on the current implementation, quality review should continue to measure at least:
audit scores must be stored per document generation
dimension-level scores and rationale must be retained
audit results must be comparable across template versions
The optimization loop should detect quality regressions and propose targeted changes.
detect regression when scores fall below prior template baseline by a configurable threshold
generate optimization recommendations with expected gain and confidence
classify recommendation type and target area
until approved unless explicitly allowed for safe automation
The improvement loop should analyze recurring failure patterns across generated documents.
identify issues appearing above a configurable frequency threshold
produce structured improvement items such as:
link recommendations back to evidence from audits or generations
, with selective automation only where confidence is high and blast radius is low.
any change with low confidence or mixed historical results
corrective patch changes with strong historical support
every applied optimization must record reviewer identity and approval time
automation policies must be configurable by template class or framework
A future-state self-healing model is feasible, but should be constrained.
Automatically apply only if all of the following are true:
expected gain exceeds a configurable threshold
predicted accuracy/confidence exceeds a configurable threshold
there is no compliance or structural impact
run verification audit on the next N generated documents
auto-revert if regression or instability is detected
flag the event for admin review
Document regeneration should be triggered by:
explicit admin or bulk regeneration request
allow targeted regeneration for impacted documents only
allow bulk regeneration for high-priority templates/projects
support dry-run evaluation before full regeneration rollout
define concurrency and throttling controls for regeneration workloads
compare new output quality against previous baselines
detect regressions at both aggregate and dimension level
prevent rollout when high-severity regressions appear
automatically revert safe-automation changes if verification fails
rollback must restore prior template version and metadata
rollback event must be audited with reason and operator
affected regenerated documents should be traceable to the reverted template version
H. Data, Audit, and Observability Requirements
To maintain the lifecycle over time, ADPA needs stronger operational visibility.
trend views by template, template family, framework, and project
safe corrective updates with immediate verification auditing
This balances self-healing capability with governance and quality control.
add richer audit metadata for approvals/rejections
persist recommendation, regression, and regeneration history
provide dashboards or reports for quality movement
expose governance and lifecycle analytics to admins
K. Template Quality Gates and Release Control
Templates must pass explicit quality gates before they become operational, shared broadly with users, or made available for bulk document generation.

No template or template version should move directly from update to production. Every new template and every new template version must re-enter a gated validation path so enhancements are proven before full release.
template created or updated but not yet available for operational use
every new version automatically falls back to this gated state until validated
validate prompt completeness, schema compatibility, required metadata, and framework bindings
block promotion if required fields, validation rules, or dependencies are missing
run the template against a defined test pack or representative sample scenarios before release
ensure the candidate version is only available in isolated test mode or to authorized reviewers
validate all quality control points across completeness, consistency, professional quality, standards compliance, accuracy, and context relevance
all defined quality gates must pass before the version can be released to full production
compare candidate results against the currently active template baseline to prove that enhancements do not introduce regressions
require measurable evidence that the new version is equal or better on mandatory quality thresholds before promotion
require explicit approval once all prior gates have passed
only after approval may the template become available to shared users, operational generation flows, and bulk document generation
a template in any quality-gate stage must not be visible as a production-ready shared template
bulk document generation must only use templates that have completed all quality-gate validations and have been explicitly released
publishing a new template version must automatically remove operational eligibility from that candidate until gate validation is complete
the previously approved production version should remain the fallback version until the replacement version fully passes release gates
every gate result must capture evidence, timestamp, reviewer or system actor, and pass/fail outcome
To support quality gates cleanly, the lifecycle model should distinguish between production-active templates and validated-but-not-yet-released candidate versions.
Templates must have explicit, auditable lifecycle states
Template and document versions must remain linked and traceable
Multi-dimensional quality audits must remain first-class signals
Regressions and recurring issues must produce structured recommendations
Only low-risk, high-confidence changes should be auto-applied
Regeneration must preserve lineage and support targeted/bulk modes
Every applied change must be reversible
Metrics, approvals, regressions, and outcomes must be queryable
The following decisions should be confirmed before full implementation:
Which template classes are eligible for automation?
What exact thresholds should define regression and confidence?
Which changes qualify as major vs minor vs patch at the document layer?
Should regeneration be immediate, scheduled, or queue-based by default?
What approval workflow is required for framework-specific templates?
Which admin roles can approve, reject, rollback, or bulk-regenerate?
ADPA already has the foundation of a sophisticated
preserve the current manual governance model for major changes
formalize lifecycle and versioning rules in a single operating model
add stronger observability and lineage tracking
introduce confidence-based safe automation only for patch-level improvements
add verification auditing and rollback to support a true self-healing loop without sacrificing control
existing backend services reviewed during analysis
WA-65 context-pipeline documentation and follow-up WA-160 for observability/persistence patterns where relevant
convert this page into an approved ADR or operating standard
break the backlog into implementation tickets
align database schema and admin workflows to the lifecycle states above
define policy thresholds for automation, verification, and rollback
